title: Factory Pattern
description: Centralize object creation so that clients don't need to know which concrete class to instantiate. Ask a Factory -- don't new it yourself.

hook.crux: Don't create objects yourself -- ask a Factory to create them for you.
hook.oneLiner: Move object creation logic into a dedicated place so adding new types doesn't ripple through every file that creates objects.
hook.problemStatement: Imagine a notification system where OrderService, PaymentService, ShippingService, and ReturnService all create notifications. Each service has if-else chains: if email, new EmailNotification(); if SMS, new SMSNotification(); if push, new PushNotification(). Now the PM says 'add WhatsApp.' You touch 10+ files. Constructor of SMSNotification changes? 10+ files again. Testing? Every service is tightly coupled to every notification type.

analogy.title: The Pizza Restaurant
analogy.description: You walk into a pizza restaurant and say 'Margherita.' You don't go into the kitchen, grab dough, spread sauce, sprinkle cheese, and fire the oven. The kitchen (Factory) takes your order (input) and hands you back a ready pizza (product). Tomorrow they add Truffle Pizza to the menu -- you don't change how you order. The kitchen handles it.

participants.name: Product Interface
participants.role: The common contract all created objects share

participants.name: Concrete Products
participants.role: The specific implementations the factory creates

participants.name: Factory
participants.role: The ONE place that decides which concrete product to create

participants.name: Client
participants.role: Code that asks the factory for objects without knowing concrete types

brainTriggers.description: When you hear these keywords in a problem statement, your brain should instantly light up with 'Factory pattern!'

triggers.example: Create a vehicle based on type -- car, bike, truck
Generate a report depending on format -- PDF, Excel, CSV
The system has multiple types of payments
Dynamically create the right handler at runtime
Client shouldn't know the exact class being instantiated

triggers.keyword: easy to add new types in the future
triggers.example: Design it so adding a new notification channel is easy

notStrategy.keyword: swap behavior at runtime on same object
notStrategy.reason: That's algorithm swapping on an existing object, not creation of new objects
Builder configures HOW to build. Factory decides WHICH type to build.

notStrategy.keyword: families of related objects that must stay consistent
notStrategy.reason: Abstract Factory creates families. Simple/Method Factory creates one product type.

brainTriggers.litmusTest: If the QUESTION is 'which class should I instantiate?' and the answer depends on runtime input, think Factory.

steps.title: Define the Product Interface
steps.description: Create the contract -- the common interface all products share. Every product type implements this. Client code only ever sees this interface.

steps.title: Create Concrete Products
steps.description: Each product type gets its own class implementing the interface. EmailNotification handles email, SMSNotification handles SMS. Each owns ONLY its logic.

steps.title: Build the Factory
steps.description: One class/method that takes a type indicator (enum, string) and returns the right concrete product through the interface. All if-else/switch creation logic lives HERE and nowhere else.

steps.title: Client Uses Factory
steps.description: Client asks the factory for an object. Client only knows the interface. Adding a new type means adding a new class + one line in the factory. Client code is UNTOUCHED.

java.title: Factory Pattern - Notification System (Java)
java.explanation: Java uses interfaces as the Product contract. Simple Factory uses switch/enum for compile-time safety. Registry Factory uses Map<Enum, Supplier> for zero if-else extensibility. Client only knows the Notification interface -- adding WhatsApp touched only the factory, not any service.

cpp.title: Factory Pattern - Notification System (C++)
cpp.explanation: C++ uses abstract classes with pure virtual functions. Smart pointers (unique_ptr) handle ownership via RAII -- no manual delete. Registry uses std::function to store creation lambdas. Virtual destructor is mandatory for safe polymorphic deletion.

kotlin.title: Factory Pattern - Notification System (Kotlin)
kotlin.explanation: Kotlin's 'object' keyword creates a singleton factory naturally. 'when' expressions are exhaustive with enums -- compiler warns if you miss a case. Constructor references (::EmailNotification) serve as factory lambdas. Clean, concise, safe.

solidConnections.connection: OrderService no longer has the responsibility of creating notifications. Factory owns creation. Service owns business logic. Each class has one reason to change.
solidConnections.example: Before: OrderService creates + sends notifications. After: Factory creates, OrderService just uses them.

solidConnections.connection: Add WhatsApp? Create a new class + add one line in factory. OrderService, PaymentService, ShippingService stay CLOSED for modification.
solidConnections.example: 10 services use NotificationFactory.create(). Adding WhatsApp changes only the factory. 10 services untouched.

solidConnections.connection: Client depends on Notification (abstraction), not EmailNotification (concretion). The factory provides the right object without the client knowing the concrete class.
solidConnections.example: OrderService holds Notification (interface). Factory decides the concrete type. OrderService is decoupled from all implementations.

solidConnections.connection: All products returned by the factory are substitutable through the interface. Email, SMS, Push all satisfy the Notification contract.
solidConnections.example: NotificationFactory.create(EMAIL) and NotificationFactory.create(SMS) both return Notification -- client uses them identically.

lldProblems.where: Vehicle creation at entry gate based on sensor data
lldProblems.why: Sensor gives a type string. Gate doesn't care about vehicle internals -- just needs a Vehicle to find the right spot and calculate rate.

lldProblems.where: Channel creation based on user preference
lldProblems.why: Multiple services send notifications. Factory centralizes creation. Add Telegram? Change factory only.

lldProblems.where: Processor creation based on payment method selected by user
lldProblems.why: Each processor has different validation and charging logic. Factory picks the right one. Use Factory Method when creation involves private config (API keys, vault credentials).

lldProblems.where: Document creation when user clicks 'New' and selects file type
lldProblems.why: User selects document type at runtime. Factory returns the right document. Adding a new format doesn't change the editor.

lldProblems.where: Ride object creation when user selects ride type
lldProblems.why: Different ride types have different capacity, pricing, and matching rules. Factory creates the right Ride object from user selection.

lldProblems.where: Logger creation based on environment configuration
lldProblems.why: Dev uses ConsoleLogger, production uses CloudLogger. Factory reads config and creates the right logger. Switch environments without code changes.

lldProblems.where: Item dispensing based on selection button/code
lldProblems.why: User presses a button, machine creates the right item. Adding new items means adding a class and registering in the factory.

lldProblems.where: Special cell creation while building the game board
lldProblems.why: Board config specifies cell types. Factory creates the right cell object for each position. Adding PowerUpCell doesn't change board setup logic.

lldProblems.where: Discount creation based on customer tier or promotion code
lldProblems.why: Promotions change weekly. Factory creates the right discount from promo code or customer tier. Cart doesn't know discount internals.

lldProblems.where: Restaurant listing strategy based on user region and preferences
lldProblems.why: Ranking algorithm varies by region and A/B test group. Factory creates the right ranker from config. Adding a new ranking algorithm is one class + one factory line.

bestFriends.relationship: Factory creates the right Strategy object. Strategy defines behavior, Factory selects which behavior to inject. They solve orthogonal problems: Factory = WHICH object, Strategy = HOW it behaves.
bestFriends.example: NotificationFactory.create('SMS') returns SMSNotification, which is then used as a Strategy by NotificationService

bestFriends.relationship: Factory Method IS Template Method + creation hook. The abstract class defines the workflow, the factory method is the abstract step that subclasses fill in.
bestFriends.example: NotificationService.send() defines the flow (create -> send -> log -> audit). createNotification() is the hook subclasses override.

bestFriends.relationship: Factory decides WHICH type. Builder decides HOW to configure it. They combine when you need to both select a type AND configure it with many options.
bestFriends.example: Factory picks EmailNotification. Builder sets subject, CC, BCC, attachments, template, locale.

evilTwin.similarity: Both are creational patterns that encapsulate object creation details from the client.
evilTwin.difference: Factory answers WHICH type to create (one decision, one line). Builder answers HOW to configure a complex object (many steps, fluent API). If you need to choose between types, Factory. If you need step-by-step construction, Builder.
evilTwin.mnemonic: Factory = WHICH type. Builder = HOW to build.
evilTwin.note: Factory returns a ready-to-use object in one call. Builder requires multiple configuration steps before build().

evolution.direction: Simple Factory evolves into Abstract Factory when you need to create FAMILIES of related objects that must be consistent (e.g., India: UPI + Rupay + GST Receipt).
evolution.when: If your factory needs multiple create methods for related products that vary together by theme/region/OS.

evolution.direction: Simple Factory evolves into Registry when you want to eliminate the if-else/switch entirely and make type registration dynamic.
evolution.when: When new types are added frequently and you don't want to modify the factory at all.

antiPatterns.description: Don't create a factory when there's only one implementation that won't change. Over-engineering with patterns is as bad as under-engineering without them.
antiPatterns.goodCode:
// Just use it directly
Logger logger = new ConsoleLogger();
antiPatterns.fix: Ask: Do I have 3+ types? Is the type chosen at runtime? Is creation logic duplicated? If NO to all, skip the factory.

antiPatterns.trap: God Factory -- knows everyone's private details
antiPatterns.description: A factory that loads SMTP config for email, API keys for SMS, and device tokens for push knows too much. It violates SRP by owning private knowledge of every product type.
antiPatterns.fix: Either let products self-configure (Option A) or use Factory Method so each subclass owns its private creation logic (Option B).

antiPatterns.trap: String keys without validation -- runtime typo bombs
antiPatterns.description: Using raw strings as factory keys means typos compile fine but crash at runtime. 'emal' compiles, but factory.create('emal') throws at runtime.
antiPatterns.badCode: Notification n = factory.create("emal"); // typo! compiles fine, crashes at runtime
antiPatterns.fix: Use enums for factory keys. Parse strings to enums at system boundaries (API, config). Strings never travel deep into code.

antiPatterns.trap: Caching mutable products -- thread safety nightmare
antiPatterns.description: Returning the same mutable object from a factory creates shared state. Thread 1 sets CC, Thread 2 sends -- OTP goes to the boss.
antiPatterns.fix: New instance for stateful products. Cache only stateless products. Use Supplier/lambda in registry for lazy creation.

antiPatterns.trap: Factory Method when there's no shared orchestration
antiPatterns.description: If the abstract class only has createProduct() and nothing else, the subclass hierarchy is pointless. Simple Factory would be cleaner.
antiPatterns.fix: Factory Method is justified ONLY when the abstract class has shared orchestration (log, retry, audit) around the created product. No shared logic = use Simple Factory.

quiz.question: A logistics system needs to create shipments via Air, Ground, or Sea based on package priority. What pattern fits best?
options: Factory -- create the right Shipment based on priority
Strategy -- swap shipment algorithm at runtime
Observer -- notify when shipment is created
Builder -- construct shipment step-by-step
quiz.explanation: 'Based on priority' = runtime type selection. 'Create shipments' = object creation. That's Factory. Strategy would be for swapping how a shipment calculates delivery time.

quiz.question: What's the key difference between Factory and Strategy?
options: Factory decides WHICH object to create. Strategy decides WHICH algorithm to run.
Factory is creational, Strategy is structural.
Factory uses interfaces, Strategy uses abstract classes.
There is no real difference.
quiz.explanation: Factory = WHICH object (creation). Strategy = WHICH behavior (algorithm). They're orthogonal -- Factory can create Strategy objects.

quiz.question: A factory loads SMTP config for email, API keys for SMS, and device tokens for push -- all inside one method. What's the problem?
options: God Factory -- it knows private details of every product type, violating SRP
Nothing wrong -- factories should know how to create everything
Should use Builder pattern instead
The factory needs more methods
quiz.explanation: The factory is a God class. Products should self-configure, or use Factory Method so each subclass owns its private creation logic.

quiz.question: When should you choose Simple Factory over Factory Method?
options: When creation is simple (just 'new X()') and you own all the code
Always -- Factory Method is outdated
When you have more than 5 product types
When products need to be cached
quiz.explanation: Simple Factory for simple creation. Factory Method when creation involves complex private knowledge per type, or when others extend your framework.

quiz.question: A factory uses String keys: factory.create('emal'). The typo compiles fine but crashes at runtime. How do you fix this?
options: Use enums instead of strings -- NotificationType.EMAIL catches typos at compile time
Add more try-catch blocks
Use Factory Method instead of Simple Factory
Add validation to check string spelling
quiz.explanation: Enums provide compile-time safety. IDE autocompletes them. Typos are impossible. Parse strings to enums at system boundaries only.

quiz.question: Does Factory pattern eliminate tight coupling?
options: No -- it MANAGES coupling by centralizing it from N places to 1 place. Someone must still call 'new'.
Yes -- Factory completely eliminates coupling between client and products
Only Factory Method eliminates coupling, Simple Factory doesn't
Coupling is eliminated when combined with Singleton
quiz.explanation: Factory doesn't eliminate 'new' -- it centralizes it. The progression: scattered (10 files) -> Factory (1 file) -> DI framework (config). Zero coupling is impossible in OOP.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: Products have different constructor parameters (EmailNotification needs SMTP host, SMS needs API key). How does a factory with a single create(type) method handle this?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: Three approaches: (A) Products self-configure internally -- factory stays clean. (B) Factory takes a generic config object. (C) Use Factory Method so each subclass owns its private creation knowledge. Option A is most common in practice.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: A Registry/Map factory creates ALL objects at startup. What if some products are expensive to create?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: Store Supplier<Notification> (lambdas/factories), not instances. The registry holds recipes, not dishes. Objects are created lazily only when requested. This is called Lazy Factory.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: Factory Method mixes creation logic with business logic in the same class. Isn't that violating SRP?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: It's intentional when creation and business logic are coupled by design (e.g., UPI service needs UPI-specific creation AND UPI-specific retry logic). If they're unrelated (OrderService creating Notifications), use a separate Factory class.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: If Factory Method subclasses decide creation, isn't the creation logic scattered across classes?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: Yes, and that's justified ONLY when each piece of scattered logic is private knowledge belonging to that class (SMTP config in EmailService, VPA validation in UPIService). If creation is simple new X(), centralize in Simple Factory.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: We still need if-else somewhere to create the concrete creator/service in Factory Method. Isn't this still tight coupling?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: Factory Method was designed for frameworks where each team directly instantiates their own creator -- no if-else needed. In standalone apps, yes, you need a Simple Factory on top to select the right service. Factory Method alone doesn't solve client-side selection.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: Factory returns an interface type. But I need type-specific methods like setCC() on EmailNotification. What do I do?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: Configure DURING creation, not after. Push type-specific configuration into the factory or use Builder before factory. If you need to cast after creation, either your interface is too thin or you shouldn't be using a factory for that use case.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: Is Factory pattern relevant in microservices where you can't 'new' a remote service?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: MORE relevant. The factory creates HTTP clients, Kafka producers, or gRPC stubs -- proxies to remote services. Client code calls sender.send() without knowing if it's in-memory or remote. Same interface, same factory, different product internals.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: I don't see abstract factory methods in my Spring codebase. Where did Factory go?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: Spring IS the factory. @Autowired replaces manual factory calls. @Bean methods in @Configuration classes ARE factory methods managed by the framework. The concept lives on; the manual pattern doesn't.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: Why not just use constructor overloading instead of a Factory?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: Constructors can only return their own type (can't return subtypes), can't have meaningful names (createForMarketing vs createForOTP), and cramming all type logic into one class creates a god-class. Factory can return any subtype through the interface.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: Static factory methods in Java (List.of, Optional.empty, Integer.valueOf) -- are they the same as Factory Pattern?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: No. Joshua Bloch's Static Factory Method is a constructor alternative for the SAME class (naming, caching, readability). GoF Factory Pattern chooses between DIFFERENT implementations at runtime. They share the name 'factory' but solve different problems.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: Static factory makes testing harder because you can't mock static methods. Did Factory pattern just make code HARDER to test?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: Yes, if the factory is static. Solution: make the factory an injectable interface. Client gets NotificationFactory injected, not a static call. In tests, mock the factory. Never use static factories in code that needs testing.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: Can Factory return Singletons? Is that safe?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: Safe ONLY for stateless products (no mutable fields). For stateful products, shared mutable state causes race conditions. Rule: stateless = cache and reuse. Stateful = new instance every time.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: Products have a deep inheritance tree (Email -> SecureEmail -> MarketingEmail). Factory explodes with types. How do you handle this?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: Use hierarchical factories (channel factory picks email/sms, then email factory picks secure/marketing) or a composite key registry with Map<(Channel, Purpose), Supplier>. Never one giant flat factory.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: Where exactly does creation END and configuration BEGIN? Whose job is configuration?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: Factory creates (WHICH type). Builder configures (HOW to set up). Client gets back a fully ready object. If the client configures after factory returns, something's wrong. Factory + Builder combine naturally.

deepThoughtQuestions.question: When should I say NO to Factory?
deepThoughtQuestions.answer: When: only 1 type, type known at compile time, only 2 stable types, creation is trivial, or you're adding it 'for the future' (YAGNI). The YAGNI test: 3+ types? Runtime choice? Complex/duplicated creation? If NO to all, skip Factory.

whenToUse: Type chosen at runtime from user input, config, or environment
Creation logic duplicated across multiple classes
You want to add new types without touching existing client code
Building a library/SDK that others extend with their own types
whenNotToUse: Type is known at compile time and is stable
Only 2 stable types -- simple if-else is fine
Creation is trivial (just new X() with no logic)
You're adding it 'for the future' that may never come

structure.Factory: One place that creates the right product (NotificationFactory)
structure.Client: Uses products through interface, never touches concrete classes

cheatSheet.sixWordSummary: Ask Factory. Don't new it yourself.
cheatSheet.codeSmellTrigger: if-else / switch on type to decide which object to new

practiceQuestions.title: Vehicle Factory for Parking Lot
practiceQuestions.description:
Create a Simple Factory for a parking lot system:
- Vehicle interface with getSize() and getHourlyRate()
- Car: MEDIUM size, $20/hr
- Truck: LARGE size, $50/hr
- Motorcycle: SMALL size, $10/hr
- VehicleFactory.create(type) returns the right vehicle
- Use enum for type safety
- Demo creating vehicles and printing their details
hints: Start with an enum VehicleType { CAR, TRUCK, MOTORCYCLE }
Vehicle interface has two methods: getSize() and getHourlyRate()
Factory uses switch/when on the enum to return the right concrete class

practiceQuestions.title: Notification System with Registry Factory
practiceQuestions.description:
Create a Registry-based Factory for notifications:
- Notification interface with send(message) and getChannel()
- Email, SMS, Push, WhatsApp implementations
- NotificationRegistry using Map<Enum, Supplier> for lazy creation
- Support dynamic registration of new types at runtime
- Demo: register a custom 'Telegram' notification without modifying factory code
hints: Use EnumMap<NotificationType, Supplier<Notification>> for the registry
Supplier.get() creates the object lazily -- only when requested

practiceQuestions.title: Payment Gateway with Factory Method + Strategy
practiceQuestions.description:
Design a payment system combining Factory and Strategy:
- PaymentProcessor interface: validate(), charge(amount), refund(txnId)
- CreditCardProcessor: Luhn validation, card network charging
- UPIProcessor: VPA validation, NPCI API charging
- Abstract PaymentService with factory method createProcessor()
- Each service subclass owns private creation logic (config loading, key management)
- Simple Factory on top to select the right service based on user choice
- Demo the full flow: user selects payment method -> factory picks service -> service creates processor -> processes payment
hints: PaymentService is abstract with createProcessor() as factory method and processPayment() as the template
PaymentServiceFactory.create(type) returns the right PaymentService subclass
This combines: Simple Factory (select service) + Factory Method (create processor) + Strategy (processor interface)

bestPractices: Use enums for factory keys -- compile-time safety, no typo crashes
Simple Factory for 80% of cases -- don't over-engineer with Factory Method unless creation is complex
Factory Method only when creation involves private knowledge per type (config loading, key management)
Store Supplier/lambdas in registry, not instances -- lazy creation avoids resource waste
Products should be self-sufficient when possible -- factory stays clean
Make factory injectable (interface, not static) for testability
Return new instances for stateful products -- never cache mutable objects
Parse strings to enums at system boundaries -- enums travel through business logic

commonMistakes.mistake: Creating a Factory for only 1 type that won't change
commonMistakes.correct: Just use 'new' directly. Add Factory when you actually have 3+ types chosen at runtime.

commonMistakes.mistake: Factory knows private details of every product (SMTP config, API keys, tokens)
commonMistakes.correct: Products self-configure, or use Factory Method so each subclass owns its private knowledge.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using raw strings as factory keys -- typos crash at runtime
commonMistakes.correct: Use enums. Parse strings to enums at API/config boundaries only.

commonMistakes.mistake: Returning cached mutable objects from factory -- thread-unsafe shared state
commonMistakes.correct: New instance for stateful products. Cache only stateless/immutable products.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using Factory Method when there's no shared orchestration in the abstract class
commonMistakes.correct: If the abstract class only has createProduct() and nothing else, Simple Factory is cleaner.

commonMistakes.mistake: Confusing Factory (WHICH type to create) with Builder (HOW to configure complex object)
commonMistakes.correct: Factory picks the type in one call. Builder configures step-by-step. Combine when you need both.
